coredata.dc:description: Clove essential oil exhibits excellent antibacterial properties; however, its application is limited by low water solubility. To overcome this, solid particle-based Pickering emulsions have been explored. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are promising solid particles for food applications, have not been studied regarding their synthesis from residual biomass for forming Pickering emulsions. This study aimed to synthesize CQDs from clove residue left over after the extraction of essential oil, assess their emulsifying efficacy, and compare their antibacterial properties with those of a traditional emulsion employing Polysorbate 80 as a surfactant. Four types of CQDs were synthesized using ultrapure distilled water and ethanol, with analyses such as 1H NMR, XPS, and interfacial tension confirming that CQDs synthesized with 40 % ethanol were the most effective as emulsifying agents. Additionally, the unique characteristics of CQDs rendered the Pickering emulsion’s surface relatively rough, which enhanced its interaction with bacteria and demonstrated potent antibacterial effects through disruption of the cell membrane. Consequently, this study suggests that the emulsifying efficiency and properties of Pickering emulsions can be tailored by controlling the surface properties of the solid particles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High-resolution transmission electron microscope images of carbon quantum dots synthesized with 40% ethanol.

1H Nuclear magnetic resonance spectra of (A) 0 %, (B) 20 %, (C) 40 %, (D) 60 % CQDs. The 0, 20, 40, and 60 % CQDs refer to CQDs synthesized using 0, 20, 40, and 60 % ethanol as the solvent, respectively.

XPS analysis of different types of (A) 0 %, (B) 20 %, (C) 40 %, and (D) 60 % CQDs, respectively. High-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s region for (E) 0 %, (F) 20 %, (G) 40 %, and (H) 60 % CQDs, respectively, the N1s region for (I) 0 %, (J) 20 %, (K) 40 %, and (L) 60 % CQDs, respectively, and the O1s region for (M) 0 %, (N) 20 %, (O) 40 %, and (P) 60 % CQDs, respectively. (Q) Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy spectra of four types of CQDs. 0, 20, 40, and 60 % CQDs refer to CQDs synthesized using 0, 20, 40, and 60 % ethanol as the solvent, respectively.

(A) Surface tension and (B) interfacial tension of CQDs. 0, 20, 40, and 60 % CQDs refer to CQDs synthesized using 0, 20, 40, and 60 % ethanol as the solvent, respectively. The concentration of CQDs was maintained at 1 mg/mL. Non-CQDs denote ultrapure distilled water used as a control. All data represent the average of three independent experiments, with error bars representing standard deviations. Different letters denote significant differences (P < 0.05).

(A) Average droplet size and (B) zeta potential of Pickering emulsion clove essential oil (PECEO) formed using CQDs. 0, 20, 40, and 60 % CQDs refer to CQDs synthesized using 0, 20, 40, and 60 % ethanol as the solvent, respectively. The concentration of CQDs was 1 mg/mL for all PECEO formations, and the ratio of aqueous phase (pH 7.0) to clove essential oil was fixed at 3:2. Data represent the mean of three independent experiments with error bars indicating standard deviations. Different letters indicate significant differences (P < 0.05).

(A) Confocal laser scanning microscope images of O/W Pickering emulsion clove essential oil (PECEO) stabilized with CQDs synthesized with 40% ethanol. (B) High-resolution transmission electron microscope image of O/W PECEO stabilized with CQDs synthesized with 40% ethanol. (C) and (D) pertain to the top section, (E) and (F) to the bottom section, (G) and (H) to the left section, and (I) and (J) to the right section of (B).

Changes in droplet size of Pickering emulsion clove essential oil (PECEO) formed using (A) 0.2 mg/mL, (B) 0.5 mg/mL, (C) 1.0 mg/mL, (D) 2.0 mg/mL, (E) 3.0 mg/mL of CQDs synthesized with 40 % ethanol after 7 days. Each graph details the size distribution of 1,000 emulsion droplets. All PECEO was formulated in a ratio of 3:2 of aqueous phase (pH 7.0) to clove essential oil. (F) Changes in interfacial tension at 40 % CQD concentration, showing the average of three independent experiments with error bars representing standard deviations. Different letters indicate significant differences (P < 0.05).

Optical microscope images of Pickering emulsion clove essential oil (PECEO) droplets. PECEO stabilized with diffetents concentrations of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). (A) and (B) represent CQDs at a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL, (C) and (D) at 0.5 mg/mL, (E) and (F) at 1 mg/mL, (G) and (H) at 2 mg/mL, (I) and (J) at 3 mg/mL. Images (A), (C), (E), (G), and (I) were captured 3 h after the formation of the PECEO, whereas images (B), (D), (F), (H), and (J) were taken after 7 days.

Time-kill curves of Pickering emulsion clove essential oil (PECEO) and Tween-80 based emulsions clove essential oil (TECEO) at various concentrations against (A) Staphylococcus aureus and (B) Escherichia coli O157:H7 are presented. PECEO was formed using 40 % CQDs at a concentration of 2 mg/mL, while TE was formed using 1 % (w/w) Tween-80. A 3:2 ratio of the aqueous phase (pH 7.0) to clove essential oil (CEO) was maintained. The control group (sonicated clove essential oil, SCEO) was prepared by dispersing CEO in ultrapure distilled water with ultrasound treatment. Data represent the mean of three independent experiments, and error bars reflect the standard deviation.

Cell membrane destruction in (A) Staphylococcus aureus and (B) Escherichia coli O157:H7 was observed after 30 min of treatment with Pickering emulsion clove essential oil (PECEO), Tween-80 based emulsion clove essential oil (TECEO) and sonicated clove esssential oil (SCEO).Fluorescence data from the treated emulsions were normalized to that of untreated emulsions. Results represent the mean of three independent experiments with error bars showing the standard deviation. Different lowercase letters signify significant differences within the same emulsion types (P < 0.05); different uppercase letters denote significant differences between emulsion types at the same concentration (P < 0.05).

Atomic force microscopy images showcase the Root Mean Square Roughness (Rq) of different emulsion types. (A) Pickering emulsion stabilized with 2 mg/mL 40 % CQDs. (B) Traditional emulsion stabilized with 1 % (w/w) Tween-80.

Fluorescence microscopy images showing bacterial adhesion to Pickering emulsion clove essential oil (PECEO) and Tween-80-based emulsion clove essential oil (TECEO): (A) Staphylococcus aureus with PECEO, (B) S. aureus with TECEO, (C) Escherichia coli O157:H7 with PECEO, and (D) E. coli O157:H7 with TECEO.

The penetration rate of clove essential oil into ULs after 30 min of treatment with. Pickering emulsion clove essential oil (PECEO) and Tween-80 based emulsion clove essential oil (TECEO). Standard deviations are based on three independent experiments. Different letters indicate significant differences (P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of clove residue-derived carbon quantum dots: Application in Pickering emulsion with enhanced antibacterial properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Clove essential oil exhibits excellent antibacterial properties; however, its application is limited by low water solubility. To overcome this, solid particle-based Pickering emulsions have been explored. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are promising solid particles for food applications, have not been studied regarding their synthesis from residual biomass for forming Pickering emulsions. This study aimed to synthesize CQDs from clove residue left over after the extraction of essential oil, assess their emulsifying efficacy, and compare their antibacterial properties with those of a traditional emulsion employing Polysorbate 80 as a surfactant. Four types of CQDs were synthesized using ultrapure distilled water and ethanol, with analyses such as 1H NMR, XPS, and interfacial tension confirming that CQDs synthesized with 40 % ethanol were the most effective as emulsifying agents. Additionally, the unique characteristics of CQDs rendered the Pickering emulsion’s surface relatively rough, which enhanced its interaction with bacteria and demonstrated potent antibacterial effects through disruption of the cell membrane. Consequently, this study suggests that the emulsifying efficiency and properties of Pickering emulsions can be tailored by controlling the surface properties of the solid particles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne diseases (FBD) represent a significant public health concern worldwide, leading to morbidity and mortality across nations and hindering economic development [1]. Typically, FBD results from contamination by bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli O157:H7 during the stages of food production, processing, transportation, and storage [2,3]. Addressing microbial contamination, the food industry often resorts to chemical preservatives such as benzoate, propionate, and nitrate. However, as consumer concerns about their safety increase, there is a growing interest within the scientific community to identify safe and effective natural antibacterial agents [4,5].

Essential oils are volatile substances produced through the secondary metabolism of plants and are classified as GRAS (Generally Recognized as Safe) by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration, making them appropriate for use in food products [6,7]. Clove essential oil (CEO) is an aromatic, volatile substance extracted from Syzygium aromaticum and primarily comprises eugenol, eugenyl acetate, β-caryophyllene, and α-humulene, with eugenol constituting over 50 % [8]. Eugenol is known to have a potent antibacterial effect against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria by compromising their cell walls and membranes, leading to the leakage of intracellular contents and disruption of normal cellular activities [9]. However, despite its impressive antibacterial potency, CEO is prone to easily deterioration. Eugenol undergoes oxidation via a 1-electron transfer pathway when exposed to light, resulting in the formation of phenoxyl radicals. Meanwhile, β-caryophyllene transforms into caryophyllene oxide upon exposure to air [10]. Additionally, the use of CEO in food products is limited due to its low water solubility [11], and prior studies have indicated that its activity can be diminished by interactions with other molecules [12,13].

Emulsions are dispersions of two immiscible, thermodynamically unstable liquids and are typically classified into water-in-oil (W/O) and oil-in-water (O/W) types. Utilizing O/W emulsions can address the limitations of essential oils and facilitate their use in food [14]. To manage thermodynamic instability and enable efficient emulsification, surfactants are employed in emulsification systems [15]. Polysorbate-80 (Tween-80), synthesized by polymerizing oleic acid onto ethoxylated sorbitan using ethylene oxide, is a common surfactant [16]. However, ethylene oxide, utilized in Tween-80 production, is classified as a Group 1 carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer, with sufficient evidence indicating a correlation with lymphoma and breast cancer in animal studies and limited evidence from human studies [18]. These concerns have prompted the exploration of natural surfactants, with ongoing studies on phospholipids, proteins, polysaccharides, and bacteria [19,20].

An emulsion stabilized with solid particles instead of classical surfactants is known as a Pickering emulsion (PE). Although a PE retains the fundamental characteristics of a traditional emulsion stabilized by surfactants, it can replace a traditional emulsion in most applications [21]. PE is noted for its superior stability because it incorporates solid particles, offering increased resistance to aggregation compared to classical emulsions [22,23]. This enhanced stability arises from the high desorption energy of the physical barrier that the solid particles form between the two liquids [24]. Additionally, as PE does not utilize traditional surfactants like Tween-80 and is highly biocompatible, it holds significant appeal in the pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and food industries, where side effects due to surfactants are a concern [21,22]. In addition to these stability benefits, PE offer practical advantages over traditional emulsions, as they require less stabilizing agent during production. The use of edible, natural solid particles further enhances their biocompatibility, positioning them promising carriers for bioactive substance delivery, particularly in applications that prioritize safety and natural composition [25].

The development of various solid particles constitutes a major research area within PE. Historically, researchers explored and developed unmodified solid particles, but recent advancements in material technology have shifted focus towards controlling particle characteristics and their application in PE [26]. Most studies have determined that the key to PE stabilization lies in the wettability of the solid particles. Particles suited for PE should possess an optimal hydrophilic and hydrophobic balance (HLB), and their effectiveness as surfactants is verified by a reduction in the interfacial tension between two liquids [27].

Currently, various physical and chemical methods are employed to produce solid nanoparticles. Nevertheless, due to high costs, energy losses, and environmental pollution associated with bio-waste, there is a growing imperative to develop more environmentally friendly methods [28]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are spherical carbon-based nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm, synthesized from organic materials containing carbon. They consist of an sp2-hybridized carbon core and sp3-hybridized chemical groups in the shell [29]. CQDs offer a distinct advantage in PE as their chemical composition and surface wettability can be easily tailored to optimize HLB values. This versatility eliminates the need to blend multiple surfactants, a step often required to achieve specific oil HLB values. For instance, CQDs can be modified with hexyl isocyanates to enhance their hydrophobicity or subjected to acidic oxidation to increase hydrophilicity [30,31]. Moreover, due to their low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness, carbon-based nanoparticles are highly suited for the pharmaceutical and food industries [32]. These characteristics indicate that CQDs are ideal as solid particles in PE and could potentially overcome the limitations associated with CEO. CQDs are produced using either a top-down method, which decomposes larger materials like nanotubes or graphite, or a bottom-up approach, employing small molecules like organic acids or amino acids as precursors [33]. Notably, the bottom-up method, which synthesizes CQDs simply and swiftly by employing food byproducts, is both eco-friendly and economical [34]. Several studies have explored the synthesis of CQDs using purified organic acids or amino acids and their application in PE [30,32,35,36]. However, to date, no studies have examined the formation of PE using CQDs synthesized from food byproducts. Due to the low yield of essential oils (0.5–8 % w/w), the essential oil industry generates significant amounts of residual biomass [37,38]. Considering economic benefits and environmental sustainability, the synthesis of CQDs represents a viable method for utilizing this residual biomass. Therefore, this study aimed to synthesize four types of CQDs using the remaining clove byproduct after essential oil extraction and to evaluate their effectiveness as solid particles for PE formation. Additionally, we examined the antibacterial effects of the Pickering emulsion clove essential oil (PECEO) stabilized by clove-derived CQDs.

The extraction of CEO followed a procedure previously reported [39]. CEO was extracted from 100 g of dried clove buds using steam distillation in a Clevenger apparatus for 6 h, employing ultrapure distilled water as the solvent. The resulting hydrosol was condensed and separated into oil and water phases by centrifugation. To remove residual moisture, anhydrous sodium sulfate (≥99.0 %, SAMCHUN, Korea) was added to the CEO. The purified CEO was then stored in a 50 mL conical tube, covered with foil, and kept at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

After oil extraction, the used clove buds were dried thoroughly in an oven, then ground into a powder using a mixer. CQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method, which allows for the use of various precursors and provides an environmentally friendly approach with high production yield [40]. Five grams of clove powder were mixed with 50 mL of ultrapure distilled water, 20 %, 40 %, and 60 % ethanol. These mixtures were homogenized for 5 min at a 24 kHz power level (350 W) using a sonicator (UMC Scientific Instruments, Korea). Subsequently, the homogenized mixture was placed in a hydrothermal synthesis autoclave reactor and heated to 200 °C for 24 h. Once the reaction concluded, the reactor was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 8,000g for 15 min. The supernatant was extracted with a syringe, filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter, and dialyzed against ultrapure distilled water over two days with water changes every 24 h. After dialysis, the CQDs were freeze-dried to obtain a powder. The CQDs synthesized using different solvent concentrations are referred to as 0 %, 20 %, 40 %, and 60 % CQDs, respectively.

Clove-derived CQDs were characterized using various analytical instruments. The 40 % CQDs were examined with a transmission electron microscope (TEM, 200 kV JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan). The chemical structuresof the CQDs were analyzed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Versaprobe Ⅱ, ULVAC-PHI, Japan), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific, USA), and Auto Sampler 500 MHz Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectrometry (NMR, 500 MHz FT-NMR spectrometer, Bruker, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of surface tension and interfacial tension curves of four types of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface tension and interfacial tension of four types of CQDs solutions were measured using a surface tensiometer (FTA 200, First Ten Angstroms, USA) via the pendant drop method. Specifically, four types of CQDs were dissolved in ultrapure distilled water to form an aqueous solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL, and the pH was adjusted to 7.0. After 10 min, the surface tension was determined by suspending 15 μL of the CQDs aqueous solution in air using a syringe with a flat needle. Interfacial tension measurements were conducted at 10-minute intervals over 1 h by introducing 15 μL of CEO from a syringe into a quartz cuvette containing 2 mL of CQDs aqueous solution (1 mg/mL concentration). For both measurements, ultrapure distilled water without CQDs served as the control.

The PECEO stabilized with four types of CQDs was prepared using the following method. First, CQDs were dissolved in ultrapure distilled water to achieve a 1 mg/mL aqueous solution and the pH was adjusted to 7.0. Subsequently, 6 mL of the CQDs aqueous solution and 4 mL of CEO were added to a 50 mL conical tube, which was then placed in ice and homogenized using a sonicator for 10 minat a power level of 24 kHz (350 W).

TEM (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan) and Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM, Nikon ECLIPSE Ti, Nikon, Japan) were used to confirm the stabilization of PECEO by CQDs. The TEM samples were prepared based on a previous study [41]. PECEO, as prepared in section 2-5, was diluted 100-fold in ultrapure distilled water. A five-microliter aliquot of this diluted solution was placed on a carbon-coated copper grid (Sigma-Aldrich, TEM-HC300-CU-UL, USA), air-dried for 10 min, and then fully dried in an oven for 24 h. The CLSM samples were prepared by diluting PECEO 100-fold in ultrapure distilled water.
The droplet size and zeta potential of PECEO formed with four types of CQDs were determined using a Zetasizer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Panalytical, USA).

To optimize the formation conditions, the concentrations of 40 % CQDs were set to 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mg/mL, and PECEO was prepared as described in section 2-5. The average droplet size changes were analyzed 3 h and 7 days post-preparation using an optical microscope (CX41, Olympus, Japan) and the Image J software. For microscopy, PECEO was diluted 100-fold prior to analysis.

S. aureus (ATCC 29,213 and ATCC 25923) and E. coli O157:H7 (ATCC 43890, ATCC 43889, and ATCC 35150)were obtained from the Bacterial Culture Collection at Seoul National University, Seoul, Korea. Each strain was individually cultured in 10 mL of tryptic soy broth (MB Cell, Kisan Bio, Korea) in a shaking incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. The cells were then harvested by centrifugation at 4,000 rpm for 20 min, washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and resuspended in PBS. Two strains of S. aureus and three strains of E. coli were combined to form bacterial cocktails, which were then used in subsequent experiments; each cocktail (S. aureus and E. coli) had a concentration of approximately
10
9
CFU/mL.

To set up the time-kill assay, 10 mL of ultrapure distilled water was put into a 15 mL conical tube, followed by the addition of various concentrations of PECEO and Tween-80-based emulsion clove essential oil (TECEO). The concentrations utilized were 5, 4, and 3 µL/mL for S. aureus and 2, 1.5, and 1 µL/mL for E. coli O157:H7. Following this, 0.2 mL of bacterial cocktail was added to each tube, and the mixture was immediately homogenized using a vortex mixer for 5 s. The experiment was carried out for 40 min, with homogenization at 5-minute intervals. At specified intervals, 0.1 mL of the treated sample was transferred to 0.9 mL of sterilized PBS, serially diluted 10-fold, and 0.1 mL of such diluted solution was plated on each selective medium. All plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and post-incubation, colonies were counted and noted as
l o g
10
CFU/mL. For S. aureus, Baird Parker Agar (MB Cell, Kisan Bio, Korea) and for E. coli O157:H7, Sorbitol MacConkey Agar (MB Cell, Kisan Bio, Korea) were used.

Cell membrane damage caused by PECEO and TECEO was quantitatively assessed using propidium iodide (PI, Sigma-Aldrich, USA). The treated samples were incubated for 30 min under the conditions detailed in sections 2–8. 1 mL of the treated sample was collected, washed twice with PBS, resuspended in PBS containing PI at a concentration of 2.9 µM, and incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. The cells were then centrifuged at 13,500 g for 10 min to obtain a pellet, washed twice with PBS, and resuspended in PBS. Fluorescence was measured using a spectrofluorophotometer (F-7100, Hitachi, Japan) at.
excitation/emission wavelengths of 493/630 nm.

The surface roughness of PECEO and TECEO was measured using an Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, Nanoman, Veeco, USA). The samples were prepared following a previously reported protocol [42]. Briefly, 10 µL of both diluted 100-fold PECEO and TECEO were applied to a mica sheet (Ted Pella, 52–15, Sweden) and dried in a drying oven for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence microscopy analysis of bacterial adhesion to emulsions
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence microscopy (Axio Imager.Z2 with Apotome.2, ZEISS, Germany) was employed to qualitatively assess bacterial adhesion to PECEO and TECEO. The emulsions were diluted 1/10 and stained with Nile Red (4 µg/mL, stock solution: 2 mg/mL in dimethyl sulfoxide). Bacteria, S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 (prepared as described in section 2.7), were separately stained with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC, 0.05 mg/mL, stock solution: 1 mg/mL in PBS) at 37 °C for 30 min. An equal volume of stained bacteria was added to the stained emulsions and incubated in the dark for 1 h at room temperature before images were acquired. Images were obtained using appropriate filter sets for Nile Red (excitation: 559 nm, emission: 635 nm) and FITC (excitation: 495 nm, emission: 519 nm).

To analyze the differences in antibacterial effects between emulsion types, unilamellar liposomes (ULs) were employed as a cell membrane model, and cell membrane permeability was assessed [43]. ULs were synthesized using lecithin (Samchun, L0197, Korea) that was dissolved in chloroform (≥99 %, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) to produce a 0.1 M solution. This solution was further diluted with 12 mL of chloroform and 2 mL of methyl alcohol (≥99.9 %, SAMCHUN, Korea) and stirred at 600 rpm using a magnetic stirrer while 80 mL of PBS was added dropwise. The solvent was then removed using a vacuum evaporator at 40 °C for 40 min. PBS was added to achieve a final volume of 50 mL, and stored at 4 °C. 5 mL of ULs and 3 mL of PECEO or TECEO (100 µL/mL concentration) were combined and stirred at 600 rpm for 30 min at room temperature. Post-treatment, 1 mL of the sample was collected and centrifuged at 13,500 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the supernatant was removed, and 1 mL of absolute ethanol (Millipore, Germany) was added to dissolve the pellet. The solution was diluted 10- or 100-fold with absolute ethanol for measuring the CEO content at 290 nm using UV/VIS spectroscopy (UVmini-1240, SHIMADZU, Japan).

Data on surface tension, average droplet size, zeta potential, time-kill curves, cell membrane destruction, and cell membrane permeability were collected in triplicate and analyzed using the Statistical Analysis System (SAS; Version 9.4, SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA). All statistical analyses utilized analysis of variance (ANOVA) and the Kruskal-Wallis test, with the Bonferroni test serving as a post-hoc analysis when Kruskal-Wallis test results were statistically significant. In all cases, a P-value of < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The TEM analysis was conducted to investigate the microstructure and morphology of CQDs. Fig. 1 (A,B) shows the TEM image of 40 % CQDs, which are uniformly distributed without agglomeration and display a spherical shape with a 0.14 nm lattice spacing, consistent with the typical characteristics of CQDs [44]. Fig. 2 (A-D) presents 1H NMR spectra for 0 %, 20 %, 40 %, and 60 % CQDs, respectively. This experiment was designed to quantitatively assess the amphiphilicity of CQDs. HLB is employed as an indicator of the amphiphilicity of nonionic surfactants [45]. For nonionic surfactants, the HLB value can be estimated using the hydrophilic to hydrophobic molecule ratio through 1H NMRanalysis [46]. In a previous study, the HLB value of paprika-derived CQDs was estimated using the following equation: HLB = 18.24R + 1.8 [47], where R is defined as ΣH(W)/(ΣH(W) + ΣH(O)). Here, H(W) represents theintensity of the integral curve of hydrophilic molecules, and H(O) represents the intensity of the integral curve of hydrophobic molecules. In Fig. 2, the integral curves with chemical shifts (δ) between 2.5 and 5 were categorized as hydrophilic molecules, while the rest were deemed hydrophobic. According to the formula, the estimated HLB values ​​of 0 %, 20 %, 40 %, and 60 % CQDs were 7.05, 7.43, 8.83, and 7.56, respectively (Table S1). Typically, when the HLB value ranges between 3.5 and 6, the surfactant can form a W/O emulsion, and when it is between 8 and 18, it can form an O/W emulsion [48]. As such, the 40 % CQDs, with an HLB value of 8.83, were identified as the most suitable solid particles for forming PECEO.

Furthermore, XPS and FTIR analyses were conducted to investigate the underlying causes of the observed changes in the HLB value of CQDs. XPS analysis revealed that all four types of CQDs exhibited C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks (Fig. 3 A-D). Fig. 3(E-H) illustrates the high-resolution C1s spectra for 0 %, 20 %, 40 %, and 60 % CQDs, respectively. For 0 % CQDs, the identified structures included: C = C (284.56 eV), C-O (285.38 eV), C-N (285.95 eV), and C = O (287.75 eV) [49–52]. For 20 % CQDs, the structures were: C = C (284.54 eV), C-O (285.61 eV), C-N (286.08 eV), and C = O (287.88 eV) [29,53,54]. For 40 % CQDs, the structures were: C = C (284.56 eV), C-O (285.79 eV), C = O (287.68 eV), and COOH (288.83 eV) [55–57]. For 60 % CQDs, the structures were: C = C (284.65 eV), C-O (285.86 eV), C-N (286.44 eV), and C = O (287.63 eV) [58–60]. Fig. 3(I-L) presents the high-resolution N1s spectra for 0 %, 20 %, 40 %, and 60 % CQDs, respectively. For 0 % CQDs, the identified structures were: Amine N (399.46 eV), Pyrrolic N (400.70 eV) and Graphitic N (401.64 eV). For 20 % CQDs, the identified structures were: Amine N (399.76 eV), Pyrrolic N (400.41 eV) and Graphitic N (401.41 eV). For 40 % CQDs, the structures were: Amine N (399.34 eV), Graphitic N (400.93 eV), Graphitic N (401.43 eV), and Pyridinic N (397.31 eV). For 60 % CQDs, the structures were: Amine N (399.53 eV), Graphitic N (401.38 eV), Pyridinic
N
+
(402.26 eV), and Pyridinic N (397.96 eV). Fig. 3(M−P) depicts the high-resolution O1s spectra for 0 %, 20 %, 40 %, and 60 % CQDs, respectively. All CQDs consist of two structures: C = O bonds (530.94/530.59/530.54/530.55 eV) and C-O or N-O bonds (532.31/532.27/532.20/532.19 eV). Fig. 3(Q) displays the FTIR spectra of the four types of CQDs. Peaks attributed to C-H (2900
c m
- 1
), C = C/C-O (1550
c m
- 1
), C = O (1710
c m
- 1
), and O-H/N-H (1030
c m
- 1
) are commonly observed in all CQDs. The = C-H (675
c m
- 1
) peak is evident in 20 %, 40 %, and 60 % CQDs. Moreover, the C≡C/C≡N (2115
c m
- 1
) peak is unique to 40 % CQDs. XPS and FTIR analyses confirmed the presence of C = C bonds in all four types of CQDs, indicating their successful synthesis [61]. Furthermore, XPS analysis revealed that the chemical composition of CQDs varies with different solvents, as detailed in Table S2. As indicated in Table S2, increasing ethanol concentration reduces the proportion of C-N bonds while augmenting the proportions of C-O and C = C bonds, aligning with previous research on CQDs synthesized using distilled water and ethanol [62,63]. XPS analysis is frequently utilized to evaluate the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of materials. Given that oxygen is more electronegative than nitrogen, it forms stronger hydrogen bonds with water molecules, enhancing hydrophilicity [64]. Additionally, COOH groups exhibit greater hydrophilicity than C-O, C-N, and C = O bonds [65]. These findings suggest that the highest HLB value in the 40 % CQDs is attributed to an increase in C-O bonds and the formation of COOH bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of surface tension and interfacial tension curves of four types of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Interfacial tension denotes the energy required to expand the surface or interfacial area between immiscible liquids, and it is a critical parameter influencing emulsion stability [66]. Given that lower surface and interfacial tensions decrease the energy required to create an emulsion, the effectiveness of an emulsifying agent is assessed by measuring these tensions [15,61]. Thus, this experiment aimed to determine whether the four types of CQDs behaved in accordance with the HLB values obtained from 1H NMR analysis.

Fig. 4 (A) shows the reduction in surface tension between ultrapure distilled water and air. As the concentration of ethanol increases, the surface tension of CQDs decreases but experiences an increase at 60 % CQDs. Specifically, for ultrapure distilled water without CQDs, the surface tension was 73 mN/m; for 0 % CQDs, it was 50 mN/m; for 20 % CQDs, 45 mN/m; for 40 % CQDs, 41 mN/m; and for 60 % CQDs, 45 mN/m. Fig. 4(B) illustrates the decrease in interfacial tension between ultrapure distilled water and CEO. Similar to Fig. 4 (A), the lowest interfacial tension occurs at 40 % CQDs, with a subsequent increase at 60 % CQDs. For ultrapure distilled water without CQDs, the interfacial tension remains approximately at 7 mN/m, whereas at 40 % CQDs, it decreases most notably. The rapid and significant decrease in interfacial tension suggests that 40 % CQDs reach the interface the most swiftly, making them the most effective emulsifying agents for PECEO formation [67,68]. Furthermore, this observation aligns with previous studies that have analyzed surface tension by altering the surface chemical composition of CQDs. Yang et al. (2023) reported that the surface tension of S-CQDs (sulfonated CQDs, S-CQDs) synthesized through a sulfonation process was lower than that of CQDs synthesized using citric acid and dodecylamine. They attributed this result to the alkyl groups formed during the sulfonation process, which endowed the S-CQDs with hydrophobicity and made them amphiphilic [61]. However, in our study, the low surface and interfacial tension at 40 % CQDs can be attributed to the increased hydrophilicity due to the formation of more C-O and COOH bonds, leading to the synthesis of amphiphilic CQDs. While 60 % CQDs demonstrate increased hydrophobicity due to a higher proportion of C = C bonds, they also exhibits elevated levels of amine N, C-O, and C = O bonds compared to the 0 % and 20 % CQDs variants. The presence of these hydrophilic groups mitigates the hydrophobic effect of the C = C bonds [69,70]. As a result, the surface tension of 60 % CQD is higher than that of 40 % CQD and is comparable to that of 20 % CQD. Thus, the results of the interfacial and surface tension measurements corroborate the HLB values of CQDs determined using 1H NMR analysis, indicating that 40 % CQDs are optimal as emulsifying agents for PECEO formation.

The HLB estimation, as well as surface and interfacial tension measurements, confirmed that 40 % CQDs are the most effective emulsion stabilizers. To further evaluate the effects of these properties on emulsion stability, the droplet size and zeta potential of PECEO were examined.

Fig. 5 (A) indicates that the droplet size of PECEO stabilized with 40 % CQDs is smaller compared to when stabilized with 0 %, 20 %, and 60 % CQDs. It is established that the stability of emulsions increases as the HLB value of the surfactant approaches that of the oil phase [71]. A previous study demonstrated that using a surfactant with an HLB value of 13.93 achieved the smallest droplet size when emulsifying CEO [72]. Although this optimal HLB value may vary based on emulsion preparation method and is not absolute, the 40 % CQDs possess an HLB value closest to those reported in the literature. Therefore, it is hypothesized that 40 % CQDs, with the HLB value closest to that of CEO, will yield the smallest droplet size in PECEO formation. For a more in-depth analysis, we indirectly estimated the adsorption energy of the four CQD types by calculating the surface excess concentration using the Gibbs adsorption equation. This calculation assumed a molecular weight of 225 kDa for the CQDs, based on previous studies [73,74]. The data are summarized in Table S3. The surface excess concentration of CQDs at the interface indicated that 40 % CQDs had the highest concentration, 1.24 ⅹ
10
- 7
mol/m2. This result is attributed to the HLB value of the 40 % CQDs being most suited to PECEO formation. Considering the relationship between CQD concentration at the interface and droplet size trends among different CQD types, it is suggested that 40 % CQDs increase the amount at the interface, promoting stable emulsion formation and maintaining smaller droplet sizes. Moreover, smaller droplet sizes enhance emulsion stability by reducing susceptibility to gravitational separation [75].

Fig. 5(B) displays the zeta potential of PECEO with various CQDs concentrations. Irrespective of the type, all absolute values are greater than 40, indicating sufficient electrostatic repulsion between droplets to prevent agglomeration [76]. These results are consistent with previous studies that showed an increase in the absolute zeta potential of silica nanoparticles as the ethanol concentration used as a solvent increased (40, 50, and 60 %) [77]. The measurements of emulsion droplet size and zeta potential confirm that 40 % CQDs generate the most stable PECEO. These findings align with the HLB values and interfacial tension measurements, further affirming that 40 % CQDs are highly effective as an emulsifying agent for PECEO.

The fundamental mechanism of PE formation involves the adsorption of solid particles at the water–oil interface [21]. To establish that 40 % CQDs serve as effective emulsifying agents, we confirmed their presence at the water–oil interface through TEM and CLSM analysis [78,79].

Fig. 6 (A) presents a fluorescence microscope image of PECEO with a circular blue fluorescent ring. CQDs smaller than 10 nm exhibit semiconducting properties due to energy level changes caused by quantum confinement effects [29]. Thus, the fluorescence in the PECEO confirms the presence of CQDs. Emulsion droplets typically assume spherical shapes to minimize their surface area [80]. Additionally, the observed reduction in interfacial tension in Fig. 4 suggests that CQDs migrate to the water–oil interface. Based on these observations, the blue fluorescent ring visually demonstrates the localization of CQDs at the water–oil interface, facilitating the formation of PECEO.

Fig. 6 (B-J) show TEM images of PECEO. Fig. 6(B) is an image of the entire PECEO, with diameter of 4–5 μm. The increased droplet size results from the agglomeration of multiple droplets during the sample drying process, which can be confirmed in the image. Fig. 6 (C, E, G, and I) are enlarged images of the top, bottom, left, and right parts of Fig. 6(B), respectively. In Fig. 6 (C, E, G, and I), the presence of spherical particles can be confirmed, particularly those clustered near the PECEO interface. Fig. 6 (D, F, H, and J) are enlarged images of Fig. 6 (D, F, H, and J), respectively. In the enlarged images, the spherical particles exhibit a lattice structure with a size of less than 10 nm. Consequently, these findings confirm that CQDs are located at the water–oil interface, contributing to the formation of PECEO.

Measuring droplet size over time is one of the simplest and most direct methods to evaluate the storage stability of an emulsion [22]. Fig. 7 (A-E) and Fig. 8 depicts the change in droplet size of PECEO with varying concentrations of 40 % CQDs. Significant changes in droplet size were observed depending on the CQDs concentration. Specifically, PECEO formulations using 40 % CQDs at concentrations of 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mg/mL resulted in average droplet sizes of 604, 768, 631, 680, and 723 nm, respectively, after 3 h. However, after 7 days, the average droplet sizes increased to 1233, 1115, 899, 796, and 961 nm, representing increases of 104 %, 42 %, 42 %, 17 %, and 33 % respectively, compared to sizes at 3 h. These changes are visually confirmed in the microscopic images presented in Fig. 8, where (A, C, E, G, I) show droplets after 3 h, and (B, D, F, H, J) show them after 7 days.

Fig. 7(F) displays the interfacial tension measurements at a 40 % CQD concentration, providing insight into how variations in CQD concentration affect droplet size. The interfacial tension decreased significantly (P < 0.05) with each incremental increase in CQDs concentration from 0.2 mg/mL to 0.5 mg/mL, from 0.5 mg/mL to 1.0 mg/mL, and from 1.0 mg/mL to 2.0 mg/mL. However, when the concentration increases from 2.0 mg/mL to 3.0 mg/mL, no significant additional reduction in interfacial tension is observed (P > 0.05). This suggests that using 40 % CQDs at a concentration of 2.0 mg/mL achieves sufficient adsorption at the interface, while at 3.0 mg/mL, some CQDs may remain unadsorbed and do not contribute further to interfacial activity.

When PECEO was formed using 40 % CQDs at a concentration of 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 mg/mL, it initially exhibited the small droplet size after 3 h. However, the droplet size increased significantly over time due to an insufficient amount of solid particles adsorbed at the interface. This insufficient CQDs coverage leads to an inhomogeneous emulsion, promoting droplet coalescence and a subsequent rapid increase in size as evident in Fig. 8(A-F) [81]. Sufficient particle coverage at the interface results in a uniform emulsion, enhancing resistance to coalescence over time (Fig. 7D, 8G, and 8H). The larger increase in droplet size observed at 3.0 mg/mL of 40 % CQDs compared to 2.0 mg/mL can be attributed to the depletion flocculation mechanism. At higher concentrations, excess CQDs might not adsorb effectively at the interface but instead create a network between emulsion droplets, which can promote emulsion instability [82]. This is clearly visible in Fig. 8(I, J), where not only did the droplet size increase significantly, but aggregation was also evident among the droplets.

Consequently, the most stable PECEO was achieved at a CQDs concentration of 2.0 mg/mL, demonstrating optimal performance as an emulsifying agent. This finding is supported by both the droplet size measurements in Fig. 7 and the microscopic observations in Fig. 8, which show the droplet size remained most stable over time at this concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial effect of PECEO stabilized with 40 % CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, we utilized 40 % CQDs as an emulsifying agent to formulate a PECEO and assessed its antibacterial effect compared to CEO in its unformulated state. Additionally, we compared the antibacterial efficacy of this PECEO with the TECEO Fig. 9 (A,B) illustrates the antibacterial effect of PECEO containing 40 % CQDs at a concentration of 2.0 mg/mL compared to TECEO, which includes Tween-80, against S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7, respectively. Building on a previous study that assessed the emulsification stability and antibacterial efficacy of CEO with Tween-80, TECEO was formulated using 1 % w/w Tween-80 [83]. In the control group(sonicated clove essential oil, SCEO), CEO was dispersed in ultra-pure distilled water using ultrasound for 10 min without CQDs or Tween-80 and applied at a concentration of 5 µL/mL; neither pathogen exhibited an antibacterial response. However, adding 40 % CQDs or Tween-80, both as emulsifying agents, induced a concentration-dependent antibacterial effect on both S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7. Specifically, when S. aureus was treated with PECEO at concentrations of 3, 4, and 5 µL/mL for 40 min, colony counts decreased by 1.8, 3.6, and 6.5 log (detection limit) units, respectively. Comparatively, TECEO at the same concentrations resulted in reductions of 0.8, 3.0, and 6.5 log10 (detection limit) units, respectively. For E. coli O157:H7, PECEO applications at concentrations of 1, 1.5, and 2 µL/mL for 40 min led to reductions of 0.6, 2.1, and 4.7 log10 units, while TECEO achieved reductions of 0.7, 1.7, and 4.0 log10 units at the same concentrations. These findings confirm that using an emulsifying agent can significantly enhance the surface area and water solubility of CEO, facilitating its diffusion onto the bacterial surface [84,85]. Additionally, PECEO exhibited a more potent antibacterial effect than TECEO, with the difference becoming more pronounced as treatment concentration and duration increased.

Through a time-kill assay, we confirmed that PECEO exhibited a superior antibacterial effect compared to TECEO. Although the exact mechanism of the antibacterial effect of essential oils remains unclear, numerous studies suggest it primarily results from the disruption of cell membranes by these oils [9,86,87]. The cell membrane serves as a barrier protecting bacteria from antibacterial agents; its destruction allows proteins and nucleic acids to leak, rendering normal cellular functions untenable [87]. Therefore, in this study, we quantified the damage to cell membranes using the PI staining method and compared the antibacterial effects of NS, PECEO and TECEO.

Fig. 10 (A) illustrates the extent of cell membrane damage in S. aureus caused by PECEO, TECEO and SCEO, while Fig. 10(B)demonstrates the damage in E. coli O157:H7. Fig. 10 reports the fluorescence values from the treated emulsions, normalized against those from the untreated emulsion. When S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 were treated with SCEO, no significant increase in cell membrane disruption was observed. However, for S. aureus, using PECEO at concentrations of 3 µL/mL or higher resulted in a significant increase in cell membrane destruction compared to the untreated control group (P < 0.05). In contrast, TECEO required a concentration of 4 µL/mL or higher to achieve a significant increase in membrane destruction compared to the control group. Moreover, at the same concentration, PECEO caused significantly greater membrane destruction than TECEO (P < 0.05). For E. coli O157:H7, both PECEO and TECEO significantly increased cell membrane destruction at concentrations of 1 µL/mL or higher (P < 0.05). Particularly, at a concentration of 2 µL/mL, PECEO was more effective in causing membrane destruction than TECEO (P < 0.05). These findings corroborate the trends observed in the time-kill assay data (Fig. 9), supporting the hypothesis that membrane disruption is a key mechanism of the antibacterial effect seen with CEOs.

In many studies, the difference in antibacterial effect based on the type of emulsion is attributed to the emulsion's surface charge. Specifically, positively charged emulsions have been reported to attach to the negatively charged bacterial surface via electrostatic attraction, enhancing the antibacterial effect [88,89]. However, since both PECEO and TECEO used in this study are negatively charged, explaining the difference in antibacterial efficacy through electrostatic interactions becomes difficult. Yu et al. (2021) conducted a study similar to ours, comparing the antibacterial effects of two emulsions with negative surface charges and found that the antibacterial effects varied based on the droplet surface morphology [85]. Surfaces with greater roughness are known to more effectively interact with bacteria [90]. Consequently, we evaluated the surface roughness of PECEO and TECEO to elucidate the cause of the observed differences in bacterial cell membrane destruction.

Fig. 11 displays AFM images of PECEO and TECEO, detailing their Root Mean Square Roughness (Rq). The analysis revealed that PECEO has an average roughness of 1.480 nm, while TECEO exhibits a significantly lower average roughness of 0.098 nm. Moreover, Yao et al. (2018) reported that Tween-80 has a smooth surface, consistent with the low surface roughness observed in TECEO [91]. The higher surface roughness of PECEO relative to TECEO is likely due to the agglomeration of nanoparticles. CQDs smaller than 10 nm, which possess a high specific surface area (200–800 m2/g), exhibit high surface energy [29,92]. Nanomaterials with higher surface energy tend to aggregate, thus forming larger aggregates that contribute to increased surface roughness [93]. Therefore, the observed differences in membrane destruction between PECEO and TECEO may be attributed to the properties of the emulsifying agents.

Fig. 12 present fluorescence microscope images illustrating bacterial adhesion to the emulsions, where green fluorescence represents FITC-stained bacteria and red fluorescence indicates Nile Red-stained emulsion droplets. Fig. 12(A) depicts S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 treated with PECEO, while Fig. 12(B) shows the same bacterial strains treated with TECEO. In the PECEO-treated samples, both S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 were observed to adhere to the emulsion droplets, as evidenced by the green fluorescent bacteria closely associated with the red fluorescent emulsion droplets. In contrast, the TECEO-treated samples showed no significant bacterial adhesion, with green and red fluorescence appearing largely separate. These observations align with our hypothesis that PECEO exhibit enhanced antimicrobial effects compared to TECEO, likely due to increased bacterial interactions facilitated by higher surface roughness. As previously note, PECEO demonstrated greater surface roughness than TECEO. The increased bacterial adhesion to PECEO observed in these images suggests a potential mechanism underlying its enhanced antimicrobial efficacy. Notably, these fluorescence microscopy are consistent with prior studies that qualitatively analyzed emulsion-bacteria adhesion on zeta potential [94]. This similarity further substantiate the validity of our finding and their interpretation within the broader context of emulsion-bacteria interactions.

Compared to TECEO, which has a smooth surface, PECEO with its relatively rough surface demonstrates enhanced bacterial adhesion and increases the local concentration of CEO around the bacteria [85]. This localized increase in CEO concentration facilitates passive diffusion [43], supported by studies indicating that essential oils or their components can traverse cell membranes to penetrate the cell interior [95,96]. Therefore, evaluating the penetration of CEO into bacterial cell membranes is critical in understanding how variations in surface roughness affect interactions between PECEO and TECEO with bacteria. However, due to the complexity of living cells, artificial cell systems are often employed for such studies [97]. We investigated interactions between cell membranes and biologically active components using a simplified model, ULs [43].

Fig. 13 shows that after 30 min of treatment, both PECEO and TECEO increase in permeability into ULs. PECEO exhibited a significantly higher absorbance (P < 0.05) of 1.34 compared to TECEO's absorbance of 0.5047. The superior permeability of PECEO, consistent with previous findings where emulsions made with silica nanoparticles and Tween-80 were tested for ULs permeability [43], thus confirms that PECEO, being relatively rougher than TECEO, adheres more effectively to bacteria and facilitates CEO diffusion into the cell membrane.

This study demonstrates the efficacy of surface-modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from clove residue as effective emulsifying agents for Pickering emulsions, as evidenced by comprehensive surface chemical analysis. CQDs used to stabilize Pickering emulsions exhibited superior antimicrobial activity compared to those stabilized with Polysorbate 80. This enhancement is attributed to the unique surface roughness and chemical functionality of CQDs, which increase their interaction with bacterial membranes, leading to more effective disruption of cell membranes. Furthermore, it can be proposed that surface modification of CQDs through heteroatomic doping to achieve a positive charge may further enhance antibacterial activity by concurrently leveraging electrostatic interactions and surface roughness. Therefore, the synthesis of CQDs from agricultural byproducts represents a promising strategy for creating high-value nanomaterials with dual functionality as emulsifiers and antimicrobial agents.

Furthermore, this study evaluated the stability of the emulsion by analyzing the change in droplet size over time relative to CQD concentration. Additionally, zeta potential measurements were conducted to confirm the presence of electrostatic repulsion, providing a critical basis for understanding the fundamental formation mechanisms and stability of PE. The PECEO developed in this study demonstrate potential applications in the food and cosmetics industries; however, further research and optimization are required to meet the specific stability criteria of each field. In conclusion, this study offers valuable insight into the formation and fundamental properties of CQDs-based PE, laying a robust foundation for future applied research across various industrial sectors.

Yong-Seok Seo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jun-Won Kang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
